content:
First, label the elements of \(M\) in increasing order by
\[
m_1=1,\;m_2=2,\;m_3=4,\;m_4=5,\;\dots
\]
In fact one checks easily that
\[
m_{2k-1}=3k-2,\quad m_{2k}=3k-1\quad(k=1,2,3,\dots).
\]
We seek \(2n\) consecutive \(m\)-’s whose sum is 300.  There are two “alignment”‐cases depending on whether the starting index \(j\) is odd or even.

Case 1: \(j=2s-1\) is odd.  Then the \(2n\) terms split into the \(n\) natural pairs
\[
(m_{2s-1}+m_{2s})+(m_{2s+1}+m_{2s+2})+\cdots+(m_{2(s+n-1)-1}+m_{2(s+n-1)})\,.
\]
But \(m_{2k-1}+m_{2k}=(3k-2)+(3k-1)=6k-3\).  Hence the total is
\[
\sum_{t=0}^{n-1}\bigl[6(s+t)-3\bigr]
=6\sum_{t=0}^{n-1}(s+t)\;-\;3n
=3n\,(2s+n-2)\,.
\]
Setting this equal to 300 gives
\[
n\,(2s+n-2)=100.
\]
Since \(n\) and \(s\) are positive integers, one checks the divisors of 100 and finds exactly two solutions:
\[
(n,s)=(2,25)\quad\Longrightarrow\quad j=2\cdot25-1=49,
\]
\[
(n,s)=(10,1)\quad\Longrightarrow\quad j=2\cdot1-1=1.
\]

Case 2: \(j=2s\) is even.  Then the pairs are
\[
(m_{2s}+m_{2s+1})+(m_{2s+2}+m_{2s+3})+\cdots+(m_{2(s+n-1)}+m_{2(s+n-1)+1})
\]
and one checks \(m_{2k}+m_{2k+1}=(3k-1)+(3k+1)=6k\).  Thus the sum is
\[
\sum_{t=0}^{n-1}6(s+t)
=3n\,(2s+n-1)
\]
and we require
\[
n\,(2s+n-1)=100.
\]
Again testing divisors yields three solutions:
\[
(n,s)=(1,50),\;(4,11),\;(5,8),
\]
corresponding to \(j=2s=100,22,16\) respectively.

In total there are five values of \(n\) for which one can find \(2n\) consecutive elements of \(M\) summing to 300, namely
\[
\boxed{\,n=1,2,4,5,10\,}.
\]

\[

We seek \(2n\) consecutive \(m\)-’s whose sum is 300.  There are two “alignment”‐cases depending on whether the starting index \(j\) is odd or even.

\[
